scene.description: Engine 181 is supplied by a hydrant and is feeding a portable monitor with two equal 2½″ lines at a commercial building. The picture has no built-in training labels. All scenario labels are generated from this JSON using overlay coordinates.

sceneElements.engine: Engine 181 positioned on the street side of a commercial building
hoses: Hydrant supply line to Engine 181 intake; not included in pump pressure calculation
Two 200′ 2½″ discharge lines from Engine 181 to portable monitor
nozzles: Portable master stream nozzle flowing 500 GPM @ 80 PSI

problems.title: Engine 181 Portable Master Stream
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying a portable monitor with two 200′ 2½″ lines. The monitor is flowing 500 GPM with a master stream nozzle pressure of 80 PSI. Use 15 PSI appliance loss for the portable monitor. What pump pressure should the operator set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss
Two equal 2½″ lines means each line carries 250 GPM
FL = C × (GPM ÷ 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds
problems.instructorExplanation: Split the total portable master stream flow between the two equal 2½″ lines. Each line carries 250 gpm. Calculate friction loss for one 200′ 2½″ line, then add nozzle pressure and portable monitor appliance loss. The hydrant supply line feeds the pump intake and is not part of the discharge pump pressure calculation.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes are calculating each 2½″ line at the full 500 gpm, adding both hose line friction losses together, forgetting portable monitor appliance loss, using the hydrant supply line in the discharge pressure math, or using the wrong nozzle pressure for the master stream.

problems.title: Engine 181 Portable Master Stream
problems.question: Engine 181 supplies the portable monitor at 600 gpm total through two equal 200′ 2½″ lines. Each line carries 300 gpm. Master stream nozzle pressure is 80 psi and portable monitor appliance loss is 15 psi. What pump pressure should you set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = 36 FL + 80 NP + 0 elevation + 15 appliance loss
problems.instructorExplanation: Only the total flow changed. Split the 600 gpm between the two equal 2½″ lines, then use one line’s friction loss in the final pump pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes are calculating each 2½″ line at the full 500 gpm, adding both hose line friction losses together, forgetting portable monitor appliance loss, using the hydrant supply line in the discharge pressure math, or using the wrong nozzle pressure for the master stream.

problems.title: Engine 181 Portable Master Stream
problems.question: Engine 181 supplies the portable monitor at 500 gpm total through two equal 250′ 2½″ lines. Each line carries 250 gpm. Master stream nozzle pressure is 80 psi and portable monitor appliance loss is 15 psi. What pump pressure should you set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = 31 FL + 80 NP + 0 elevation + 15 appliance loss
problems.instructorExplanation: Only the hose length changed. The monitor is still fed by two equal 2½″ lines, so each line carries 250 gpm. Use one line’s friction loss.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes are calculating each 2½″ line at the full 500 gpm, adding both hose line friction losses together, forgetting portable monitor appliance loss, using the hydrant supply line in the discharge pressure math, or using the wrong nozzle pressure for the master stream.

problems.title: Engine 181 Portable Master Stream
problems.question: Engine 181 supplies the portable monitor at 500 gpm total through two equal 200′ 2½″ lines. Each line carries 250 gpm. Master stream nozzle pressure is 80 psi and portable monitor appliance loss is 25 psi. What pump pressure should you set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = 25 FL + 80 NP + 0 elevation + 25 appliance loss
problems.instructorExplanation: Only the appliance loss changed. The friction loss stays 25 psi, but the portable monitor loss increases from 15 psi to 25 psi.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes are calculating each 2½″ line at the full 500 gpm, adding both hose line friction losses together, forgetting portable monitor appliance loss, using the hydrant supply line in the discharge pressure math, or using the wrong nozzle pressure for the master stream.
